title: Calisthenics Program
description: Master bodyweight exercises and functional movements progressing to advanced skills like handstands, muscle-ups, and human flags.
overview: This calisthenics program teaches you to become proficient with your bodyweight, building strength, control, and athletic movement patterns. No gym required - train anywhere, anytime. Progression: basic movements → strength building → advanced skills.

weekly_schedule.exercises: Push-ups (5x8-12), Pike Push-ups (4x6-8), Dips (4x6-10), Diamond Push-ups (3x8), Pseudo Planche Leans (3x10sec)
Squats (5x15-20), Bulgarian Split Squats (4x8 each), Pistol Squat Progressions (3x5 each), Lunges (3x10 each), Hollow Body Hold (3x20sec)
Pull-ups (5x5-8), Chin-ups (4x6-8), Inverted Rows (3x8), L-Sit Progression (3x15sec), Scapular Pulls (3x10)
Handstand Hold (5x30sec), Wall Walks (3x5), Handstand Walk Attempts (3x10m), Pike Hold (3x20sec), Crow Pose (3x15sec)
10 rounds: 5 pull-ups + 10 push-ups + 15 squats. Rest 90sec between rounds
Muscle-up progressions, Front Lever progressions, Back Lever progressions, Flag holds
Full body stretching, foam rolling, yoga poses

nutrition.content: 3 whole eggs + 2 slices whole wheat toast + 1 banana + 1 tbsp peanut butter
200g chicken + 200g white rice + vegetables + olive oil

tips: Master the basics before progressing - perfect your form
Handstands: practice 10-15 minutes daily against a wall
Use progression ladders - don't jump to advanced moves too quickly
Rings are a great tool - they provide instability for strength gains
Pull-up bar and dip station are minimal equipment investments
Consistency beats intensity - 4-5 quality sessions per week
Progressive calisthenics takes time - patience is key
Film yourself to check form and track progress
Incorporate skill work separate from strength sessions
Recovery is crucial - don't do upper body pull and push same day
Flexibility is as important as strength in calisthenics
Join a calisthenics community for motivation and form checks
Progression: reps → pause reps → archer variations → one-arm variations
Practice skills when fresh - do skill work at the start of workouts
